request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"knewton::edtech","name":"knewton","one_liner":"Knewton was a US-based adaptive learning platform (founded 2008) that sold AI-driven personalization infrastructure to enterprise publishers and universities, raising ~$157M before being acquired by Wiley in 2019 for under $17M — a fraction of capital raised.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Knewton operated as an enterprise B2B SaaS platform licensing adaptive-learning APIs to publishers and institutions on recurring subscriptions. The new pitch is a direct-to-consumer tiered freemium app with small-group tutoring sessions — fundamentally different customer acquisition logic, pricing architecture, and revenue mix."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both target the K-12 and/or higher-ed learning market using AI/adaptive personalization. Knewton's trusted facts place it in 'adaptive learning platform,' directly overlapping with the core technology proposition. However, Knewton focused on the US market while the pitch targets Indonesia and the Philippines — distinct geographies with different willingness-to-pay and infrastructure profiles."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Knewton's go-to-market relied on long-cycle enterprise partnerships with Pearson, Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, and universities. The new pitch targets individual consumers (students/parents) directly via an app with freemium conversion — a consumer growth/viral GTM, not an institutional sales motion."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Knewton raised over $150M across six rounds and operated for 11 years, suggesting a mature scale stage at failure. The pitch appears to be an early-stage concept. Some overlap in the challenge of scaling AI personalization infrastructure, but at very different capital and operational scales."}},"why_similar":"Both ventures center on AI-driven adaptive learning for K-12 students. Knewton pioneered the use of real-time student performance data to personalize content recommendations — the same core value proposition the new pitch names ('AI tutoring'). Both face the challenge of demonstrating measurable learning outcomes to justify premium pricing, and both operate in an edtech market where content quality, engagement, and retention are chronic weak points. The adaptive/personalization technology thesis is directly shared.","where_diverged":"At least four concrete dimensions diverge: (1) Geography — Knewton is US-based (confirmed by Trusted facts); the pitch targets Indonesia and the Philippines, markets with lower household income, mobile-first connectivity, and different language/curriculum requirements. (2) Customer type — Knewton's Trusted facts classify it as 'enterprise'; the pitch is consumer-facing, selling directly to students and parents. (3) Business model — Knewton used B2B recurring subscription licensing to publishers/institutions; the pitch uses tiered freemium with a hybrid live small-group-session component. (4) Monetization mix — Knewton had no freemium or live-tutoring layer; the pitch blends asynchronous AI with synchronous human instruction, which changes the unit economics entirely.","failure_causes":["Overreliance on enterprise publisher partnerships with long sales cycles and slow revenue ramp","Capital-intensive infrastructure build without matching monetization velocity (raised ~$157M, sold for <$17M)","Pivot from B2C test-prep origin to B2B API platform diluted brand and product focus","Failure to demonstrate direct student-outcome ROI convincing enough for sustained institutional renewals","Market consolidation by large publishers who could build or acquire competing adaptive tools internally","Unit economics gap: high cost to build and maintain adaptive content graphs vs. subscription revenue per seat"],"lessons_for_input":["Validate willingness-to-pay in your specific geographies (Indonesia, Philippines) before scaling AI infrastructure — enterprise edtech pricing assumptions from the US market do not transfer.","Design the freemium conversion funnel around a measurable learning outcome (e.g., grade improvement, exam score) so you have a concrete upsell trigger, not just engagement metrics.","Keep the live small-group sessions as a margin-preserving differentiator from day one — pure AI tutoring platforms commoditize quickly once larger players adopt similar models.","Avoid deep dependency on any single content publisher or institutional partner for distribution; Knewton's Pearson partnership created leverage that shifted over time to the partner's favor.","Control burn relative to revenue traction: Knewton raised over 9x its eventual acquisition price — ensure your capital raises are tied to repeatable unit-economic milestones, not just technology milestones."]}
